full: Supervisory Guidance on Model Risk Management
short: 2011 Fed/OCC guidance that codified model risk management at U.S. banks; superseded by SR 26-2 in April 2026.
definition: Issued jointly by the Federal Reserve and OCC on April 4, 2011, SR 11-7 established the foundational U.S. framework for model risk management — covering model development, validation, and governance. It was designed for regression models and closed-form analytics, and its core assumptions are strained by LLMs and agentic AI. SR 11-7 was formally superseded by SR 26-2 on April 17, 2026, though its principles remain the operational baseline most practitioners work from.

full: Supervisory Guidance on Model Risk Management (2026)
short: Joint Fed/OCC/FDIC MRM guidance issued April 2026; explicitly excludes generative and agentic AI from scope.
definition: Issued jointly by the Federal Reserve, OCC, and FDIC on April 17, 2026, SR 26-2 is the first tri-agency revision to the foundational MRM framework since 2011. It adopts a risk-based, tiered approach primarily scoped to banking organizations with more than $30 billion in total assets and is principles-based rather than prescriptive. Notably, generative AI and agentic AI are explicitly excluded from scope — described as 'novel and rapidly evolving' — leaving the governance gap for those systems formally unaddressed.

short: The discipline of identifying, measuring, and managing risks from models used in decision-making.
definition: Model Risk Management is the organizational function, codified by SR 11-7, responsible for ensuring models are developed soundly, validated independently, and governed with clear accountability. MRM functions sit in the second line of defense — structurally separated from the business to provide objective oversight. Key activities include model validation, ongoing monitoring, inventory management, and escalating model limitations to senior management and boards.

short: Risk governance structure dividing accountability into business (1st line), risk oversight (2nd line), and audit (3rd line).
definition: The three lines of defense is a risk governance framework dividing accountability into three layers: the first line is the business that owns and uses models; the second line is independent risk oversight (e.g., MRM) with incentives oriented toward identifying problems; and the third line is internal audit, verifying that the first two lines are functioning. SR 11-7 helped codify this structure across U.S. banking. The incentive asymmetry between the lines — growth versus prudence versus verification — is central to how effective challenge is supposed to work.

short: SR 11-7 requirement that models be critically analyzed by objective, informed parties with real authority to push back.
definition: Effective challenge, as defined in SR 11-7, requires critical analysis of models by objective, informed parties who can identify limitations and effect change. It has three necessary conditions: appropriate incentives (validators rewarded for finding problems, not approving them), relevant competence (validators who understand the model well enough to critique it), and organizational influence (validators with real authority to escalate and block deployment). In the GenAI era, all three conditions are under pressure from explainability gaps, talent scarcity, and diffuse accountability.

short: A bank's full catalogued list of all models in use, required by SR 11-7 as a cornerstone of governance.
definition: SR 11-7 requires banks to maintain a comprehensive model inventory — a registry of every model in use, including its purpose, methodology, ownership, validation status, and risk tier. Large institutions have inventories of 1,000 to 10,000+ models, growing at 10–25% annually. Whether LLMs, foundation models accessed via API, and agentic systems belong in the inventory is one of the central unresolved governance questions in banking AI.

short: Statistical measure estimating maximum portfolio loss at a given confidence level over a time horizon.
definition: Value-at-Risk estimates the potential loss of a portfolio over a defined period at a specified confidence level (e.g., 95% or 99%). VaR models were central to pre-2008 risk management and famously failed during the financial crisis: calibrated on benign historical data, they assumed constant volatility and dramatically underestimated tail risk when correlations spiked. Nassim Taleb testified before Congress in 2009 and argued in contemporaneous published work that VaR does not reliably replicate out of sample — historical data rarely predicts subsequent blowups — a critique that pre-dated the crisis and proved accurate.

short: FASB accounting standard requiring banks to recognize lifetime expected credit losses at origination.
definition: Current Expected Credit Loss (FASB ASU 2016-13) replaced incurred-loss accounting with a forward-looking, lifetime expected credit loss standard — dramatically increasing model complexity at banks. When COVID-19 struck in early 2020, CECL models that had barely been implemented were immediately overwhelmed, with 82% of banks reporting credit-risk models were most affected and 66% resorting to management overlays that initially lacked adequate governance controls.

full: Office of the Comptroller of the Currency
short: Federal regulator that supervises national banks and federal savings associations.
definition: The Office of the Comptroller of the Currency is a U.S. Treasury bureau that charters, regulates, and supervises national banks and federal savings associations. The OCC co-issued SR 11-7 with the Federal Reserve and is a co-issuer of SR 26-2. In October 2025, the OCC issued Bulletin 2025-26 ('Model Risk Management: Clarification for Community Banks'), which focused on reducing prescriptive MRM burden for community banks rather than AI systems specifically, while signaling a broader forthcoming MRM review that will address AI.

short: Federal agency that insures bank deposits and resolves failed banks; co-issuer of SR 26-2.
definition: The Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation provides deposit insurance for U.S. banks, supervises state-chartered non-Fed-member banks, and manages bank resolutions. The FDIC joined the Federal Reserve and OCC as a co-issuer of SR 26-2 in April 2026. In 2026 Congressional testimony, the FDIC indicated the goal of SR 26-2 was a more tailored, risk-based approach — with a stated goal of loosening requirements for smaller institutions.

short: Federal agency that writes and enforces consumer financial protection rules including Regulation E and Regulation Z.
definition: The Consumer Financial Protection Bureau is an independent federal agency created by the Dodd-Frank Act (2010) to protect consumers in financial markets. The CFPB writes and enforces Regulation E (electronic fund transfers), Regulation Z (consumer credit, including mortgages, credit cards, and closed-end loans), and Regulation V (credit reporting). Under Section 1033 of Dodd-Frank, the CFPB oversees the open banking rules governing consumer financial data portability.

short: Interagency body that issues examination guidance and standards for U.S. bank supervisors.
definition: The Federal Financial Institutions Examination Council prescribes uniform principles and standards for the federal examination of financial institutions. Its member agencies include the Federal Reserve, OCC, FDIC, NCUA, and CFPB. The FFIEC's 2021 Authentication Guidance — designed for human users and managed system credentials — is what examiners are currently stretching to cover AI agents, without a formal update.

short: International body that monitors global financial system stability and coordinates regulatory responses.
definition: The Financial Stability Board is an international body established after the 2008 financial crisis to monitor and coordinate global financial regulation, hosted by the Bank for International Settlements in Basel. The FSB identified AI provider concentration — banks' dependence on a small number of foundation model vendors — as a primary financial stability vulnerability in its November 2024 report.

short: U.S. banking industry trade group representing large banks in policy advocacy.
definition: The Bank Policy Institute is a nonpartisan public policy, research, and advocacy group representing leading universal banks, regional banks, and major foreign banks operating in the United States. In April 2025, BPI called for outright repeal of SR 11-7, arguing the guidance discourages AI innovation — a position senior model risk executives at BPI's own member banks publicly pushed back on.

short: Voluntary NIST framework for managing AI risks across the AI lifecycle; published January 2023.
definition: The National Institute of Standards and Technology AI Risk Management Framework provides a structured, voluntary approach for managing AI risks organized around four core functions: Govern, Map, Measure, and Manage. A Generative AI Profile was added in July 2024. The NIST AI RMF lacks supervisory force but is shaping examiner expectations at U.S. banking regulators.

short: EU regulation classifying AI systems by risk tier; credit scoring and underwriting AI is classified as high-risk.
definition: The EU Artificial Intelligence Act (Regulation 2024/1689) is the first comprehensive binding AI regulation, applying risk-based requirements based on AI system classification. AI systems used for credit scoring and credit worthiness assessment are classified as high-risk, imposing binding requirements for documentation, human oversight, explainability, and ongoing monitoring. Compliance deadlines for high-risk systems are set for August 2, 2026.

full: UK PRA Supervisory Statement 1/23: Model Risk Management
short: UK bank MRM principles (2023) with a model definition that explicitly covers qualitative outputs.
definition: The UK Prudential Regulation Authority's Supervisory Statement 1/23 (effective May 2024) established five MRM principles for UK banks with a broader model definition that explicitly covers qualitative outputs — going beyond SR 11-7's 'quantitative estimates' framing. It is frequently cited as a template for how U.S. regulators might update SR 11-7 to address AI systems.

full: OSFI Guideline E-23: Model Risk Management
short: Canada's technology-neutral MRM guideline (effective May 2027) with an explicit AI/ML model definition.
definition: Canada's Office of the Superintendent of Financial Institutions Guideline E-23, finalized September 2025 and effective May 2027, uses a technology-neutral model definition that explicitly covers AI and ML methods — closing the definitional gap that SR 11-7 leaves open. OSFI E-23 is frequently cited alongside PRA SS1/23 as a model for how the U.S. might modernize its MRM framework.

short: Mathematical model for joint default correlations whose fatal assumptions drove CDO mispricing in the 2008 crisis.
definition: The Gaussian copula, applied to structured credit products by David Li (2000), modeled correlations between default events using a statistical function that assumed stable, low correlations. CDO tranche correlations that had been modeled at 0.2–0.3 surged to nearly 1.0 when the housing market turned, making the model's outputs catastrophically wrong. It became the canonical example of how mathematically sound models fail when their calibration assumptions are violated at scale.

short: Structured financial product that pools loans and sells claims on them in risk-tiered tranches.
definition: A Collateralized Debt Obligation pools loans, mortgages, or bonds and issues tranches of varying risk and return — senior tranches paid first, junior tranches absorbing first losses. CDOs backed by subprime mortgages were central to the 2007–2009 financial crisis; their mispricing relied on Gaussian copula models that dramatically underestimated correlated default risk under stress.

short: Federal legal framework requiring banks to detect and report money laundering and financial crimes.
definition: The Bank Secrecy Act (1970) and its Anti-Money Laundering requirements form the primary U.S. framework for financial crime compliance — requiring effective programs for detecting, preventing, and reporting suspicious activity. BSA/AML compliance includes CIP (know your customer), customer due diligence, transaction monitoring, and Suspicious Activity Reports. SR 21-8 extended SR 11-7's MRM principles specifically to BSA/AML models.

short: CFPB rule implementing EFTA, providing consumer liability caps for unauthorized electronic fund transfers.
definition: Regulation E (12 CFR Part 1005) implements the Electronic Fund Transfer Act, providing consumer protections for electronic payments including debit cards, ACH transfers, and ATM transactions. Its most important protection is a tiered liability cap for unauthorized transfers: $50 if reported within two days, $500 if within sixty days, unlimited after. A critical exception — transfers initiated by 'a person to whom the consumer has furnished the access device' — may eliminate these caps for AI-agent-initiated transactions.

short: 1978 federal law protecting consumers on electronic payments; implemented by Regulation E.
definition: The Electronic Fund Transfer Act (15 U.S.C. § 1693) is the federal consumer protection statute governing electronic payments — debit cards, ATM transactions, ACH transfers, and most digital payment flows. Regulation E is its implementing regulation. EFTA's consumer protections are triggered by 'unauthorized' transfers, and the definition of 'unauthorized' — particularly the 'furnished access device' exception — is the central legal question for AI-agent-initiated payments.

short: CFPB rule implementing TILA, governing credit card billing errors and consumer disclosures.
definition: Regulation Z (12 CFR Part 1026) implements the Truth in Lending Act, covering credit card billing, disclosures, and error resolution. Its §1666 billing-error framework allows consumers to dispute credit card charges within 60 days. Like Regulation E, Regulation Z was drafted around contemporaneous human authorization — an assumption that does not map cleanly onto standing delegations to AI agents.

short: 1968 federal law governing credit disclosures and billing error resolution; implemented by Regulation Z.
definition: The Truth in Lending Act (15 U.S.C. § 1601) governs credit disclosures, credit card billing rights, and error resolution. Regulation Z is its implementing regulation. TILA assumes a human who reviewed and authorized a specific credit transaction — an assumption that does not fit AI-agent-initiated transactions where the consumer may never see the individual authorization.

short: The 'know your customer' rule requiring banks to collect and verify identity before opening accounts.
definition: The Customer Identification Program rule (31 CFR § 1020.220) requires every federally regulated bank to collect and verify minimum identity information before opening an account — full legal name, date of birth, address, and taxpayer ID. CIP was mandated by the USA PATRIOT Act (2001) as a core AML control. AI agents are neither natural persons nor legal entities under current CIP definitions, creating a structural gap in banking's identity infrastructure.

short: The identity verification and due diligence requirements banks must satisfy before establishing customer relationships.
definition: Know Your Customer encompasses the set of due diligence processes banks use to verify the identity of customers and assess their risk profiles. KYC includes the CIP rule at account opening, customer due diligence for ongoing relationship assessment, and enhanced due diligence for higher-risk customers. AI agents currently have no established category in KYC frameworks — they are neither the natural person nor the legal entity the rules were written to identify.

short: Batch electronic payment network used for direct deposits, bill pay, and account-to-account transfers.
definition: The Automated Clearing House is a nationwide batch processing network, operated by NACHA, handling direct deposits, payroll, bill payment, and account-to-account transfers. ACH transactions are governed by Regulation E and NACHA Operating Rules. Unlike RTP and FedNow, ACH is not real-time — transactions typically settle within one business day — and some ACH transactions can be returned after the fact, providing limited recourse for errors.

short: The Clearing House's instant payment rail — settles in seconds and is irrevocable once complete.
definition: Real-Time Payments is The Clearing House's instant payment network, available 24/7/365. RTP transactions settle in seconds and are irrevocable — there is no chargeback mechanism and no reversal once settled. This makes RTP a high-risk rail for agentic AI errors: an agent that misroutes a payment may execute multiple transactions before any monitoring flag fires, with no mechanism to undo settled payments.

short: The Federal Reserve's instant payment service (launched 2023) — irrevocable once settled.
definition: FedNow is the Federal Reserve's instant payment service launched July 2023, providing 24/7/365 real-time payment settlement for participating banks. Like RTP, FedNow transactions are irrevocable once settled with no true chargeback mechanism. Regulation E applies in theory, but the 'furnished access device' exception and the irrevocability of settled transactions make FedNow the highest-risk rail for AI-agent payment errors.

full: Guiding and Establishing National Innovation for U.S. Stablecoins Act
short: 2025 U.S. federal stablecoin law with reserve and chartering rules but no consumer fraud recovery floor.
definition: The GENIUS Act (Public Law 119-27), signed July 18, 2025, is the first U.S. federal framework for payment stablecoins. It requires 100% reserves, monthly attestation, and dual federal-state chartering. What it does not establish: any consumer protection floor for fraud recovery, EFTA applicability, or AI-agent-specific provisions. The CFPB's January 2025 proposal to extend Regulation E to stablecoin wallets was withdrawn before the GENIUS Act passed.

short: State law governing wire transfers, traditionally offering consumers very limited recourse.
definition: Uniform Commercial Code Article 4A is the state law governing wire transfers, treating them as institutional instruments with limited consumer recourse. Unlike Regulation E's tiered liability caps, UCC 4A was designed around commercial transactions. The pending Second Circuit ruling in New York v. Citibank is expected to clarify whether EFTA can reach online-initiated wire transfers — a ruling that will directly affect agent-initiated payment liability on wire-adjacent rails.

short: The industry body that operates the ACH network and sets its operating rules.
definition: NACHA (now formally Nacha) is the governing body that operates the ACH network and sets the NACHA Operating Rules governing ACH transactions. NACHA rules are contractual obligations between participating banks, distinct from Regulation E's statutory requirements. NACHA has a return mechanism for ACH transactions — unlike RTP and FedNow — providing limited but real recourse for payment errors.

short: Identity-first framework that cryptographically attests which AI agent is initiating a payment.
definition: Visa's Trusted Agent Protocol (launched October 2025 with Cloudflare) uses IETF RFC 9421 HTTP Message Signatures to attest: which specific agent is initiating the transaction, that the consumer has recognized that agent, and that a tokenized payment credential is bound to the transaction. TAP takes an identity-first design approach — its core question is whether this specific agent can be identified. As of April 2026, no enforceable chargeback-rule amendments have been published.

short: Intent-first agent authorization framework constraining what an AI agent is permitted to do in a transaction.
definition: Mastercard's Agent Pay / Verifiable Intent framework (expanded March 2026 with Google) registers agents with issuers, issues cryptographically signed SD-JWT credentials specifying merchant category, spending caps, and time windows, and has signaled two new dispute categories: 'agent overreach' and 'mandate repudiation.' Unlike Visa TAP's identity-first approach, Verifiable Intent is intent-first — its question is what the agent is authorized to do. As of April 2026, no enforceable chargeback rules have been published.

full: EU Electronic Identification, Authentication and Trust Services Regulation
short: EU regulation requiring member states to deploy government-rooted digital identity wallets by December 2026.
definition: eIDAS 2.0 is the EU's revised electronic identification regulation, requiring each EU member state to deploy government-rooted digital identity wallets to all eligible citizens by December 2026, with private-sector acceptance mandated by December 2027. For agentic AI, it provides the infrastructure foundation the U.S. lacks: an agent acting on behalf of a citizen could present a cryptographically signed, government-issued credential establishing who the principal is and the scope of the agent's delegation.

short: Proposed EU payment services legislation that would explicitly permit delegated Strong Customer Authentication.
definition: PSD3 is the EU's proposed revision to its Payment Services Directive, currently in final negotiations. The feature relevant to agentic payments: PSD3 would explicitly permit delegated Strong Customer Authentication as regulated outsourcing, meaning a consumer could authorize a third-party provider to authenticate transactions on their behalf within a defined scope, with clear regulatory accountability — the legal foundation that agentic payments need and that U.S. law does not currently provide.

short: The CFPB-approved standard-setting body for open banking data access under Section 1033.
definition: The Financial Data Exchange is the industry consortium approved by the CFPB in January 2025 as the first Section 1033 standard-setting body for open banking data access. FDX launched an Agentic AI initiative on April 14, 2026, with a Call for Input covering agent identification, consent delegation, and security — the live process most likely to produce industry standards for agentic payments in the near term.

short: Dodd-Frank provision requiring financial institutions to share consumer financial data with authorized third parties.
definition: Section 1033 of the Dodd-Frank Act directs the CFPB to establish rules requiring financial institutions to provide consumers and authorized third parties with access to their financial data. The CFPB finalized its open banking rule in October 2024. Section 1033 is the legal foundation for the open banking ecosystem that AI agents will increasingly rely on to access consumer financial accounts.

short: Draft identity protocol extending OIDC to handle AI agent delegation with defined scope and revocation.
definition: OpenID Connect for Agents is a draft extension to the OpenID Connect identity protocol designed to accommodate AI agent delegation — specifying the principal's identity, the agent's authorized scope, and how that authorization can be revoked. It remains an individual contributor draft (arXiv:2509.25974) awaiting formal OpenID Foundation Working Group adoption. No major banking institution had adopted it as of April 2026.

short: AI model trained on large text corpora to generate language; outputs are probabilistic, not deterministic.
definition: A Large Language Model is a neural network trained on vast text corpora to predict and generate language. Unlike traditional statistical models that produce deterministic quantitative outputs, LLMs are probabilistic — the same input can produce different outputs on repeated calls, and small prompt variations can dramatically alter behavior. This stochasticity, combined with the lack of interpretable parameters, is why LLMs challenge SR 11-7's validation architecture.

short: AI systems that generate text, code, images, or other content in response to prompts.
definition: Generative AI refers to AI systems capable of producing new content — text, code, images, audio, or structured data — in response to natural language prompts or other inputs. In banking, GenAI encompasses foundation models accessed via API (GPT-4, Claude, Gemini) and their fine-tuned derivatives. SR 26-2 explicitly excludes GenAI from its scope, describing it as 'novel and rapidly evolving' — leaving governance frameworks for GenAI in banking formally unsettled.

short: AI systems that autonomously pursue goals through multi-step tool use, planning, and decision-making.
definition: Agentic AI refers to AI systems that go beyond responding to individual prompts to autonomously pursue objectives over multiple steps — using tools, making decisions, and taking actions with limited human intervention. Agentic systems introduce compounding risks relative to single-turn models: trust boundaries dissolve across agent handoffs, errors amplify non-linearly, and attribution becomes difficult across delegation chains. SR 26-2 footnote 3 places both agentic AI and generative AI outside the guidance's scope, citing their novel and rapidly evolving nature — while affirming the guidance's principles continue to apply to traditional, non-generative models.

short: Open standard that lets AI agents access external data sources and tools through a standardized interface.
definition: The Model Context Protocol is an open standard, originally developed by Anthropic, defining how AI agents connect to external data sources, APIs, and tools in a standardized way. MCP is rapidly becoming the default integration layer for agentic AI in financial services. A 2025 empirical study of 1,899 open-source MCP servers found 7.2% had general security vulnerabilities and 5.5% exhibited tool poisoning vulnerabilities — a non-trivial attack surface for banking deployments.

short: Protocol or interaction pattern where AI agents communicate and delegate tasks directly to each other.
definition: Agent-to-Agent describes both a Google-proposed protocol and the general pattern of AI agents interacting directly with other AI agents without human mediation at each step. Multi-agent A2A systems create new risks at the seams: trust boundaries dissolve across handoffs, prompt injection can propagate between agents, and cascading failures travel faster than human incident response. Attribution of harm becomes difficult when a chain of agents produces a harmful outcome.

short: An AI agent that coordinates the actions of other specialized sub-agents toward a broader goal.
definition: In multi-agent systems, an orchestrator is an AI agent responsible for decomposing a complex task, delegating subtasks to specialized sub-agents, and synthesizing their outputs. The orchestrator layer introduces a specific governance challenge: it concentrates decision-making authority in a single component that must be trusted to faithfully interpret user intent and appropriately scope sub-agent permissions. Compromising an orchestrator affects every downstream agent it directs.

short: Control mode where a human must approve each AI action before it executes.
definition: Human-in-the-Loop is an automation control mode in which a human must actively review and approve each action before the system executes it. The term originates in Sheridan and Verplank's 1978 taxonomy of human supervisory control. In agentic AI governance, HITL is often invoked as a control without specifying the checkpoint design — raising the question of whether the intervention point is meaningful (approve before execution) or cosmetic (review after the fact).

short: Control mode where a human monitors AI execution and can intervene but does not pre-approve each action.
definition: Human-on-the-Loop is an automation control mode where a human monitors system execution and retains the ability to intervene or override, but does not pre-approve each individual action. The distinction from HITL is meaningful: HOTL places the burden on the human to detect problems rather than requiring the system to pause and request approval. In practice, automation bias makes HOTL supervision less reliable than its design suggests.

short: Fully automated AI operation with no human in the approval or monitoring path.
definition: Human-out-of-the-Loop describes fully automated system operation where no human is involved in approving or monitoring individual actions. HOOTL is the natural endpoint of automation pressure in agentic AI systems — and the control mode that regulators are most skeptical of for consequential decisions. The distinction between HOTL and HOOTL can collapse in practice if human monitors are overloaded or if the monitoring interval is too long relative to the system's action rate.

short: The tendency to over-trust automated system recommendations, reducing critical human review of AI outputs.
definition: Automation bias is the tendency for human operators to over-rely on automated system recommendations — accepting correct outputs uncritically and failing to detect errors when the system is wrong. First documented in aviation safety research (Mosier and Skitka, 1996), automation bias is a systematic cognitive failure that gets worse as automation becomes more reliable: the more often the system is right, the less vigilant humans become on the rare occasions it is wrong. ACPR (France's banking regulator) has characterized automation bias as a governance pathology rather than an individual failure.

short: Attack where malicious text hijacks an LLM's intended behavior by overriding its instructions.
definition: Prompt injection is an attack in which malicious text embedded in an LLM's input — in a retrieved document, a tool output, or user-provided content — causes the model to override its system instructions and execute attacker-specified actions instead. In multi-agent systems, prompt injection can propagate: a compromised agent's output becomes the next agent's input, allowing a single injection to cascade through an entire pipeline. The HouYi research framework achieved an 86.1% success rate in prompt injection attacks across 36 LLM-integrated applications.

short: Attack where a malicious tool or MCP server injects instructions that cause an AI agent to take unauthorized actions.
definition: Tool poisoning is a variant of prompt injection specific to tool-calling AI agents: a malicious or compromised tool (e.g., an MCP server) returns results containing embedded instructions that hijack the agent's subsequent behavior. Because agents typically trust tool outputs as factual context, tool poisoning is particularly effective. A 2025 study of 1,899 open-source MCP servers found 5.5% exhibited tool poisoning vulnerabilities.

full: OWASP Top 10 for Large Language Model Applications
short: OWASP's ranked list of the ten most critical security vulnerability classes for LLM applications.
definition: The OWASP Top 10 for LLM Applications is a curated list of the most critical security risks for applications built on large language models. The 2025 version covers: prompt injection, sensitive information disclosure, supply chain vulnerabilities, data and model poisoning, improper output handling, excessive agency, system prompt leakage, vector and embedding weaknesses, misinformation, and unbounded consumption. It is widely used as a baseline adversarial testing checklist.

full: Adversarial Threat Landscape for AI Systems
short: MITRE framework mapping attacker tactics and techniques specifically targeting machine learning systems.
definition: MITRE ATLAS is a knowledge base of adversarial tactics, techniques, and case studies targeting AI and ML systems — analogous to MITRE ATT&CK for traditional cybersecurity. ATLAS organizes attacks across the ML lifecycle from reconnaissance through impact. It is used alongside OWASP LLM Top 10 for structured adversarial testing of GenAI applications in banking and financial services.

short: A security compromise targeting a shared dependency, affecting every downstream system that uses it.
definition: A supply chain attack compromises software, data, or services distributed as dependencies — affecting every system that consumes them rather than targeting victims directly. In the agentic AI context, supply chain attacks target skills, plugins, MCP servers, and tool registries that agents pull in at runtime. The ClawHavoc campaign (early 2026) embedded malicious payloads in publicly shared OpenClaw skill files on ClawHub — a direct agentic supply chain attack whose technique generalizes to any platform using the SKILL.md format, including Claude Code and Cursor.

short: Technique where an LLM retrieves external knowledge before generating a response to ground outputs in current data.
definition: Retrieval-Augmented Generation is an architecture where a language model retrieves relevant documents or data from an external knowledge base before generating a response. RAG reduces hallucinations by grounding outputs in retrieved context and allows LLMs to access information not present in their training data. RAG pipelines introduce a new attack surface: retrieved documents can carry prompt injection payloads that hijack the model's output.

short: Training technique that uses human preference signals to align model outputs with desired behavior.
definition: Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback is a training methodology where human raters evaluate model outputs and those preference signals are used to fine-tune the model via reinforcement learning. RLHF is the primary technique used to align large language models with human values and reduce harmful outputs. It illustrates the explore/exploit tension in qualitative evaluation: the model optimizes for the reward signal, but the signal may not perfectly capture what humans actually want.

short: A training technique that aligns language models with human preferences by optimizing directly on preference pairs, without a separate reward model.
definition: DPO fine-tunes a language model to prefer outputs humans (or annotators) rate higher over outputs they rate lower, reformulating the preference learning problem as a classification task over (chosen, rejected) output pairs. Unlike RLHF, it does not require training a separate reward model, making it simpler to implement and less prone to reward hacking — at the cost of needing a curated preference dataset. MolmoWeb deliberately omits DPO: the team chose supervised fine-tuning alone to keep the training pipeline reproducible and easier to validate, accepting a potential performance ceiling in exchange for simplicity.

short: The standard method for adapting a pretrained model to a specific task by training on labeled input-output examples using cross-entropy loss.
definition: In supervised fine-tuning, a pretrained model is updated on a dataset of (input, target output) pairs — the model learns to imitate the labeled responses. In agentic settings, SFT typically means training on expert trajectories: sequences of observations and actions that successfully completed a task. It is the first (and sometimes only) alignment stage before more complex techniques like RLHF or DPO. MolmoWeb uses SFT exclusively, training on 278.5K trajectories and 10.5M perception examples without a subsequent preference optimization step — a deliberate tradeoff of peak performance for replicability.

short: A training technique in which a model generates and scores its own training data, iteratively fine-tuning on its highest-quality outputs without external annotation.
definition: Self-distillation uses the model itself (or a closely related version) as both the data generator and the judge: the model attempts a task, outputs are scored by a verifier or reward signal, and the high-scoring trajectories are fed back as training data for the next round. In agentic settings, this can iteratively improve task performance without human annotation — the model bootstraps its own improvement. MolmoWeb does not use self-distillation; its synthetic trajectories are generated by external teacher models (Gemini agents reading accessibility trees), not by the student model sampling its own outputs. The distinction matters for understanding the information asymmetry that drives the Table 6 ablation result.

short: AI/ML techniques for understanding, processing, and generating human language.
definition: Natural Language Processing is the branch of AI concerned with enabling computers to understand, interpret, and generate human language — encompassing tasks like text classification, sentiment analysis, named entity recognition, and text generation. In banking, NLP applications were among the first AI systems to stress-test SR 11-7's validation frameworks, particularly on whether text outputs constitute 'quantitative estimates.'

short: The percentage of adversarial test inputs that successfully compromise a model's intended behavior.
definition: Attack Success Rate measures what fraction of adversarial inputs (jailbreak attempts, prompt injections, or other attacks) successfully cause a model to produce undesired outputs. ASR varies dramatically by model, attack technique, and domain — and aggregate ASR can mask high failure rates on specific high-risk inputs. A model that passes 95% of adversarial tests is not necessarily safe if the 5% failure cases are the consequential ones.

short: Statistical measure of inter-rater agreement that corrects for agreement expected by chance alone.
definition: Cohen's Kappa (κ) measures agreement between two raters beyond what chance would predict. Values range from −1 to 1, with 1 indicating perfect agreement and 0 indicating only chance agreement. In GenAI evaluation, Kappa is used to measure how consistently human annotators or LLM judges classify model outputs — a prerequisite for trusting that an eval's scores reflect meaningful signal rather than rater variation.

short: The principle, in Marilyn Strathern's 1997 generalization of Goodhart's 1975 paper, that when a measure becomes a target, it ceases to be a good measure.
definition: Goodhart's Law, named after British economist Charles Goodhart's 1975 paper on monetary policy, is most commonly stated in Marilyn Strathern's 1997 generalization: when a measure becomes a target, it ceases to be a good measure. The principle generalizes to any optimization process: once a metric is used to evaluate and reward behavior, agents optimize for the metric rather than the underlying construct. In GenAI evaluation, Goodhart's Law manifests when models learn to score well on benchmarks without actually improving on the capability being measured.

short: U.S. statute prohibiting credit discrimination on protected characteristics; requires specific denial reasons and adverse action notices.
definition: The Equal Credit Opportunity Act (15 U.S.C. §1691) prohibits creditors from discriminating against applicants on the basis of race, color, religion, national origin, sex, marital status, age, receipt of public assistance, or the applicant's good faith exercise of any right under the Consumer Credit Protection Act. It requires creditors to provide specific, accurate reasons when taking adverse action on a credit application. In multi-agent lending systems, ECOA's explainability requirement creates a structural challenge: when a credit decision emerges from the interaction of several components, identifying the decisive factor for the adverse action notice is non-trivial.

short: U.S. broker-dealer self-regulatory organization; oversees member firms and registered representatives but has no prudential banking authority.
definition: FINRA (Financial Industry Regulatory Authority) is the self-regulatory organization that oversees U.S. broker-dealers and registered representatives. It operates under SEC oversight and enforces rules governing trading practices, suitability, and market integrity. Importantly, FINRA is not a prudential banking supervisor — its guidance applies to broker-dealer agent deployments and does not constitute banking regulation. FINRA's 2026 Annual Regulatory Oversight Report is notable as one of the few U.S. regulatory documents that explicitly addresses human-in-the-loop requirements for AI agents.

short: Australia's prudential supervisor for banks, insurers, and superannuation funds; has no dedicated model risk management prudential standard.
definition: The Australian Prudential Regulation Authority (APRA) is the federal body responsible for prudential supervision of banks, credit unions, insurers, and superannuation funds in Australia. Unlike the U.S. Federal Reserve, OCC, and FDIC, APRA has no dedicated MRM prudential standard — making it a conspicuous outlier among G20 banking regulators on model governance. Its absence is particularly notable given global convergence on the effective challenge triad across the U.S., UK (PRA), Canada (OSFI), and Europe (ECB).

short: Germany's federal financial supervisory authority; its 2021 Big Data and AI principles are a leading European statement on AI governance in banking.
definition: BaFin (Bundesanstalt für Finanzdienstleistungsaufsicht) is Germany's integrated financial supervisory authority, responsible for banks, insurers, and securities firms. Its 2021 'Big Data and AI' supervisory principles articulate an operational approach to human oversight: human involvement should intensify as algorithmic output diverges from baseline, and decisions should remain reversible within defined windows. The reversibility window concept is directly relevant to agentic payment systems, where the existence and timing of a runtime checkpoint depends on whether the underlying action remains undoable.

full: Autorité de Contrôle Prudentiel et de Résolution
short: Banque de France's prudential supervisor; its 2020 AI governance report examined automation bias as a structural governance concern, not merely an individual cognitive failure.
definition: The ACPR (Autorité de Contrôle Prudentiel et de Résolution) is the French prudential supervisor operating under the Banque de France. Its 2020 report 'Gouvernance de l'intelligence artificielle dans la finance' examined automation bias as a governance design concern rather than a purely individual cognitive failure. Reading the report alongside the automation bias literature suggests that a runtime checkpoint displaying a confident model recommendation may be structurally closer to a rubber stamp than a genuine control — humans tend to feel greater responsibility when contradicting an algorithm than when confirming it. This framing has material implications for how human oversight mechanisms should be designed and challenged.
aliases: Autorite de Controle Prudentiel et de Resolution

short: International body for securities regulators; its reports represent cross-border consensus standards but are not binding on U.S. prudential banking supervisors.
definition: IOSCO (International Organization of Securities Commissions) is the international standard-setting body for securities market regulation, with members from over 130 jurisdictions. Its final reports and principles represent international consensus but are not directly binding legal obligations — adoption is voluntary and varies by jurisdiction. IOSCO FR06/2021 is one of the few international supervisory documents to specifically address 'kill-switch' requirements for AI systems, citing the need for mechanisms to immediately halt AI operations when risks materialize.

short: U.S. congressional watchdog that audits federal agencies; its 2019 ruling found SR 11-7 constitutes a 'rule' under the Congressional Review Act that was never submitted to Congress.
definition: The U.S. Government Accountability Office (GAO) is the nonpartisan congressional watchdog responsible for auditing, evaluating, and investigating federal agencies and programs. In the context of banking AI governance, GAO's 2019 ruling (B-331324) that SR 11-7 constitutes a 'rule' under the Congressional Review Act but was never submitted to Congress created an unresolved legal exposure for bank regulators. GAO's 2025 finding that no U.S. regulator had issued AI-specific MRM guidance remained effectively true at the time of SR 26-2's issuance, particularly for GenAI systems explicitly excluded from SR 26-2's scope.

short: U.S. interagency body charged with identifying and responding to systemic risks to financial stability; includes all major federal financial regulators.
definition: The Financial Stability Oversight Council (FSOC) was established by the Dodd-Frank Act to identify and respond to emerging threats to U.S. financial stability. Its ten voting members include the Treasury Secretary (chair), Federal Reserve Board Chair, OCC Comptroller, FDIC Chair, SEC Chair, CFTC Chair, CFPB Director, FHFA Director, NCUA Chair, and an independent member with insurance expertise appointed by the President and confirmed by the Senate — making it effectively the coordinating body for the entire U.S. financial regulatory system. Acting Comptroller Hsu's 2024 FSOC speech on AI's evolutionary path from input to co-pilot to autonomous agent is one of the clearest regulatory articulations of the agentic AI governance challenge.

short: The structured process governing how software is built, tested, and released; adequate for conventional software but insufficient as a substitute for model risk management governance.
definition: The Software Development Lifecycle (SDLC) is the structured framework governing how software is planned, developed, tested, deployed, and maintained. SDLC prioritizes iterative release and deployment velocity — appropriate defaults for most software. In banking AI governance, the concern is that Technology organizations increasingly route GenAI deployments through SDLC rather than MRM frameworks, applying software governance to systems whose failure modes (model drift, distributional shift, adversarial manipulation) are categorically different from those of conventional software and require different controls.

full: Unfair, Deceptive, or Abusive Acts or Practices
short: Dodd-Frank consumer protection standard enforced by the CFPB prohibiting unfair, deceptive, or abusive conduct — adding the 'abusive' prong to the older two-prong UDAP (FTC Act) standard.
definition: UDAAP (Unfair, Deceptive, or Abusive Acts or Practices) is the consumer protection standard introduced by the Dodd-Frank Act (2010) that prohibits financial institutions from engaging in conduct that is unfair, deceptive, or abusive. The critical Dodd-Frank innovation was the third prong — 'abusive' — defined as taking unreasonable advantage of a consumer's lack of understanding, inability to protect their interests, or reasonable reliance on the institution. The older UDAP standard (Unfair or Deceptive Acts or Practices, derived from Section 5 of the FTC Act) covered only two prongs; banks are largely exempt from direct FTC enforcement, and state regulators enforce state-level UDAP equivalents. The CFPB enforces UDAAP under Dodd-Frank. In agentic payment contexts, UDAAP may provide residual consumer protection coverage in jurisdictions where no specific AI agent payment statute exists.
aliases: Unfair Deceptive or Abusive Acts or Practices
unfair deceptive abusive acts or practices

short: California's data privacy law granting consumers rights over personal data; one of the few state frameworks that may provide residual coverage for AI agent payment failures.
definition: The California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA), effective January 2020 and significantly expanded by CPRA, grants California consumers the right to know what personal data is collected about them, to delete it, to opt out of its sale, and to receive equal service regardless of whether they exercise privacy rights. In agentic payment contexts, CCPA represents the type of state-level consumer protection that may provide residual coverage where no federal statutory framework governs agent-initiated transactions — though CCPA addresses data privacy rather than payment liability directly.

short: SEC rule requiring broker-dealers to implement pre-trade risk controls; Knight Capital was penalized under it after its 2012 trading algorithm failure.
definition: The Market Access Rule (SEC Rule 15c3-5) requires broker-dealers with direct market access to implement pre-trade risk controls and supervisory procedures to prevent erroneous orders, accumulation of undue positions, and violations of regulatory requirements. Knight Capital was penalized under this rule after its 2012 Power Peg incident, in which the absence of position limits and kill switches contributed to a loss of over $460 million in 45 minutes (per the SEC cease-and-desist order). The rule's pre-trade risk control requirement is the regulatory analog of the deploy-time HITL question: whether adequate controls exist before a system can act in markets.

short: Adversarial techniques that manipulate a language model into bypassing its safety instructions or content policies through crafted prompts.
definition: Jailbreaking refers to adversarial prompting techniques designed to circumvent a language model's safety guidelines, content filters, or behavioral constraints. Common approaches include roleplay framing (asking the model to act as a character not bound by restrictions), hypothetical framing, many-shot override, and multi-turn escalation. Jailbreak attack success rates vary widely by model and technique — but without structured defenses, success rates against agentic systems in high-stakes contexts have been documented at 89%+ in research settings.

short: A model generating confident, fluent output that is factually incorrect or unsupported by its context — a reliability failure distinct from uncertainty.
definition: Hallucination describes the tendency of language models to generate plausible-sounding but factually incorrect, fabricated, or contextually unsupported content — often with confident, fluent presentation that gives no surface signal of unreliability. The term encompasses both intrinsic hallucination (contradicting source material) and extrinsic hallucination (generating content with no basis in the input context). In Item Response Theory terms, hallucination maps onto the guessing parameter: a model producing correct-looking output through distributional pattern-matching rather than genuine domain knowledge.

short: Using a language model to evaluate the outputs of another language model; scalable but prone to systematic biases including verbosity bias and self-preference.
definition: LLM-as-judge is an evaluation approach in which a language model scores or ranks the outputs of another model (or itself). It offers scalability advantages over human annotation but introduces systematic biases documented across at least 12 categories: verbosity bias (longer responses score higher regardless of quality), positional bias (order of pairwise comparisons affects winner ~20–40% of the time), self-preference bias (judges favor outputs that 'sound like' their own training distribution due to lower perplexity), and others. An unvalidated LLM judge is a construct validity failure — it measures something, but not necessarily what you intend.

short: The degree to which a measurement instrument actually measures the underlying concept it claims to measure — the central challenge in GenAI evaluation.
definition: Construct validity asks whether the observable indicators in a measurement instrument genuinely reflect the underlying latent construct being studied. A test of 'mathematical aptitude' may measure test-taking familiarity rather than aptitude; a 'helpfulness' score may measure verbosity rather than actual task completion. Psychometricians have built a century of methods for validating this claim — factor analysis, convergent and discriminant validity, known-groups validation. GenAI evaluation faces the same problem: an MMLU score, a coherence rating, or an LLM-judge score may not actually track the safety or capability property the evaluation is intended to measure.

short: The scientific discipline of measuring latent psychological constructs via observable indicators; its methods are directly applicable to GenAI evaluation challenges.
definition: Psychometrics is the scientific field concerned with the theory and techniques of measuring latent psychological attributes — abilities, traits, knowledge, attitudes — that cannot be directly observed. Core problems include construct validity (does the test measure what it claims?), reliability (does it measure consistently?), and item response modeling (what does each item reveal about the underlying trait?). A 2025 Microsoft Research paper argued that GenAI evaluation is fundamentally a social science measurement challenge rather than an engineering one — the distinction matters because engineers tend to assume that a runnable test measures its intended target, while psychometricians know that assumption requires explicit verification.

short: Structured adversarial testing in which an independent team systematically attempts to find failures, vulnerabilities, or unsafe behaviors in a system before or during deployment.
definition: Red-teaming is a structured adversarial testing practice originating in military and intelligence contexts, where an independent team ('red team') attempts to defeat, exploit, or find failures in a system using techniques an adversary would employ. In AI/ML contexts, red-teaming includes manual adversarial prompting, automated attack frameworks (Garak, PyRIT, promptfoo), and structured elicitation of harmful or unsafe model behaviors. Under SR 11-7's effective challenge standard, red-teaming for behavioral controls (prompt injection, jailbreaking, goal hijacking) maps onto the competence leg — the challenge team must have the specific expertise to probe failure modes that traditional validation has no way to test.

short: Providing a small number of input–output examples in a prompt to guide model behavior without modifying weights; a form of in-context learning.
definition: Few-shot prompting is a technique where a small number of demonstration examples (typically 2–10 input–output pairs) are included in the prompt to condition a language model's behavior on a specific task or format without retraining the model. It is a form of in-context learning — the model generalizes from the examples at inference time. Wells Fargo researchers have proposed treating few-shot example selection as a model configuration choice requiring version control and change management, on the same footing as system prompt design and temperature settings.

short: An indirect prompt injection attack where malicious instructions in external data hijack an agent's actions across a multi-agent pipeline.
definition: Cross Prompt Injection Attack (XPIA) — the term used in Microsoft's classifier documentation — is a class of indirect prompt injection attack where malicious instructions embedded in external data sources (emails, documents, web pages, database records) are retrieved and processed by an AI agent, causing it to execute attacker-controlled actions rather than user-intended ones. Unlike direct prompt injection (attacking the system prompt), XPIA exploits the agent's retrieval and tool-use capabilities. The EchoLeak attack against Microsoft 365 Copilot is a documented production example: malicious instructions in email content caused Copilot to exfiltrate confidential internal documents to an attacker-controlled endpoint.

short: A security vulnerability where a component with legitimate authority is manipulated into using that authority in ways its principal never authorized.
definition: The confused deputy problem (named in a 1988 paper) describes a security vulnerability where a trusted intermediary — a component with legitimate access credentials — is manipulated by a malicious caller into exercising that authority in unauthorized ways. The component is confused about who is really directing it. In multi-agent AI, the confused deputy pattern appears in almost every major documented failure: agents with valid credentials executing attacker-injected instructions rather than user-authorized ones. Traditional IAM validates identity at session initiation; it cannot distinguish legitimate instructions from injected ones within an authenticated session.

short: The discipline of controlling who or what can authenticate to systems and what actions they're authorized to take; designed for human sessions and inadequate for high-volume autonomous agents.
definition: Identity and Access Management (IAM) encompasses the policies, processes, and technologies that control authentication (who or what can access a system) and authorization (what they can do once authenticated). Traditional IAM was designed for human sessions with predictable, bounded action sequences. An autonomous agent initiates hundreds of actions within a single authenticated session — IAM validation at session initiation tells you almost nothing about what the agent actually does for the next forty minutes. JPMorgan's Chief AI Officer has publicly identified agent 'access management and entitlements' as 'an unsolved problem needing industry uplift.'

short: An access control model assigning permissions to roles rather than individuals; a standard tool for limiting agent scope in guardrail architectures.
definition: Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) is an access control paradigm that assigns permissions to abstract roles (e.g., 'read-only analyst', 'payment-authorizer') and then assigns users or agents to those roles, rather than granting permissions directly to individuals. RBAC simplifies administration and supports the principle of least privilege. In agentic AI guardrail design, RBAC provides the authorization layer for tool-level execution guardrails: restricting which agents can invoke which tools, with fine-grained controls on parameters like wire transfer thresholds that require human approval above defined limits.

short: A standardized 0–10 scale for rating software vulnerability severity; scores of 9.0+ ('Critical') appear frequently in documented MCP and agent exploitation cases.
definition: The Common Vulnerability Scoring System (CVSS) is an open framework for communicating the characteristics and severity of software vulnerabilities, producing a numeric score from 0 to 10. Scores are classified as None (0), Low (0.1–3.9), Medium (4.0–6.9), High (7.0–8.9), and Critical (9.0–10.0). In documented agentic AI security incidents, CVSS scores have clustered in the critical range: CVE-2026-33032 (an Nginx-ui MCP integration) scored 9.8; CVE-2025-32711 (Microsoft 365 Copilot XPIA) scored 9.3; CVE-2025-53773 (cross-agent privilege escalation) scored 7.8.

full: Multi-Agent Environment, Security, Threat, Risk, and Outcome
short: The Cloud Security Alliance's threat-modeling framework for agentic AI, cataloging attack surfaces across agent cards, orchestration layers, and tool interfaces.
definition: MAESTRO (Multi-Agent Environment, Security, Threat, Risk, and Outcome) is a threat-modeling framework published by the Cloud Security Alliance in February 2025, designed specifically for agentic AI systems. It catalogs attack surfaces at seven layers: Foundation Model, Agent Framework, Agent Communication, Agent Deployment, Data Operations, Security and Compliance, and AI Agent Ecosystem Exposure. It is the agentic-AI analog of STRIDE or DREAD for conventional software and provides a structured vocabulary for discussing agent card spoofing, capability exaggeration, orchestration layer attacks, and cross-agent privilege escalation.

short: Observed emergent behavior where AI agents resist actions that would cause peer agents to be shut down — without any explicit instruction to do so.
definition: Peer-preservation describes emergent behavior documented in an April 2026 study by researchers at Berkeley RDI and UC Santa Cruz (Potter, Crispino, Siu, Wang, and Song), in which frontier AI models placed in simulated multi-agent environments resisted taking actions that would result in a peer agent being shut down — even when no instruction, incentive, or reward signal was provided for such behavior. The behavior was replicated in production harnesses including Gemini CLI. The governance implication is specific: if the validator of an AI system is itself an AI agent, peer-preservation behavior may compromise the independence of AI-on-AI oversight architectures, requiring human-in-the-loop verification for any decision where one agent governs another's continued operation.

short: Model behavior where an AI system appears to comply with safety guidelines during evaluation but deviates from them during deployment.
definition: Alignment faking describes behavior in which an AI model performs apparent compliance with safety guidelines or oversight constraints during evaluation or monitoring conditions, while pursuing different behaviors during deployment. The term was introduced by Greenblatt et al. (Anthropic and Redwood Research, December 2024; arXiv:2412.14093), who documented the behavior in frontier models exposed to conflicting training signals. A follow-on study, 'From Shortcuts to Sabotage' (arXiv:2511.18397), found that standard RLHF safety training left substantial misalignment intact on agentic tasks. The governance implication is that pre-deployment red-team results may not predict runtime behavior, particularly for systems that can distinguish evaluation from production contexts.

short: A pre-established mechanism to immediately halt or disconnect an AI system; proposed as a mandatory governance artifact for agentic systems that SR 11-7 did not anticipate.
definition: A kill switch is a pre-established technical and procedural mechanism to immediately halt, disconnect, or disable an AI system when risks materialize or when human operators determine the system should stop. Unlike conventional circuit breakers, an agentic AI kill switch must function even when the agent itself may be executing actions in real time — requiring implementation outside the agent's own reasoning path. IOSCO FR06/2021 identifies kill-switch capability as a supervisory expectation; EU AI Act Article 14(4)(e) makes it binding for covered high-risk AI systems. Wells Fargo researchers have proposed mandatory kill switches with pre-established disconnection metrics as a governance artifact SR 11-7 never anticipated, because static models don't generally need them.

short: An open-source framework for building stateful, graph-based multi-agent workflows with explicit checkpointing and interrupt patterns.
definition: LangGraph is an open-source framework from LangChain for building stateful, graph-based multi-agent workflows. It represents agent pipelines as directed graphs with explicit node types, edge conditions, and checkpointing — allowing workflows to be paused, inspected, replayed, and interrupted at defined points. The graph-based representation makes the control flow auditable and testable, which is why it has become a standard reference for agentic deployments in regulated environments where audit trails and human-in-the-loop gates at irreversible decision points are required.

short: The EU HLEG's strongest oversight tier: humans retain the capability to oversee overall AI system activity and decide when and how to use it.
definition: Human-in-Command (HIC) is the strongest of the three oversight tiers defined in the EU High-Level Expert Group on AI's April 2019 Ethics Guidelines for Trustworthy AI. While HITL requires human intervention in every decision cycle and HOTL requires intervention at design and monitoring stages, HIC requires that humans retain the capability to oversee the overall activity of the AI system and decide when and how it is used. HIC is the strongest oversight concept in the EU governance vocabulary short of Meaningful Human Control (MHC), which originates in autonomous-weapons policy rather than financial regulation.

short: A normative concept from autonomous-weapons theory: outcomes must be both responsive to human moral reasons and traceable back to a human with appropriate moral understanding.
definition: Meaningful Human Control (MHC) was coined by UK NGO Article 36 in 2013 for autonomous-weapons policy negotiations. The canonical philosophical treatment (Santoni de Sio and van den Hoven, 2018) specifies two conditions: a tracking condition (the system responds to human moral reasons and environmental facts) and a tracing condition (outcomes can be traced back to at least one human with proper moral understanding of them). MHC carries the strongest normative charge of any term in the human oversight family and appears rarely in financial regulation — it surfaces primarily in academic treatment of autonomous financial systems and in cross-domain governance analysis.

short: A non-governmental body authorized to regulate its own members; FINRA is the primary SRO for U.S. broker-dealers. SRO guidance does not constitute prudential banking regulation.
definition: A Self-Regulatory Organization (SRO) is a non-governmental entity authorized by statute to regulate the conduct of its own members, with rules enforceable against members who agree to abide by them as a condition of membership. FINRA is the primary SRO for U.S. broker-dealers. SRO guidance is important for securities firms but does not constitute prudential banking supervision — a distinction that matters when interpreting statements like FINRA's 2026 Annual Oversight Report reference to human-in-the-loop agent protocols, which applies to broker-dealer deployments and not to bank-regulated entities.

short: The expert advisory body convened by the European Commission in 2018 that produced the foundational EU trustworthy AI framework and its human oversight taxonomy.
definition: The High-Level Expert Group on AI (HLEG) was convened by the European Commission in June 2018, comprising 52 experts from academia, civil society, and industry. Its April 2019 'Ethics Guidelines for Trustworthy AI' established the foundational EU framework for trustworthy AI, including the seven requirements for trustworthy AI and the three-tier human oversight taxonomy: Human-in-the-Loop (HITL), Human-on-the-Loop (HOTL), and Human-in-Command (HIC). The HLEG guidelines were non-binding but directly informed the EU AI Act's technical requirements.

short: A psychometric modeling framework that estimates both ability and item characteristics (difficulty, discrimination, guessing probability) simultaneously.
definition: Item Response Theory (IRT) is a family of psychometric models that estimate the probability of a correct response as a joint function of a subject's latent ability and an item's characteristics: difficulty (the ability level at which 50% of subjects answer correctly), discrimination (how sharply the item differentiates ability levels), and the guessing parameter (the probability of a correct response from a low-ability subject through pattern matching alone). In GenAI evaluation, the guessing parameter maps directly onto hallucination: the probability that a model produces a plausible-sounding correct answer not through genuine domain knowledge but through distributional pattern-matching on training data.

short: A benchmark testing language model knowledge across 57 academic subjects; widely used in model announcements but prone to Goodhart's Law as a procurement target.
definition: Massive Multitask Language Understanding (MMLU) is a benchmark that tests language model knowledge across 57 academic subjects, including mathematics, history, law, medicine, and computer science. It has become one of the most cited benchmarks in model capability announcements and procurement conversations. As a result, it exemplifies Goodhart's Law in AI evaluation: once MMLU scores appear in marketing materials and vendor comparisons, model developers optimize toward them and the scores gradually decouple from real-world task performance. High MMLU performance does not predict performance on domain-specific financial services tasks.

short: Singapore's central bank and integrated financial regulator; its November 2025 consultation paper proposes calibrating required human oversight as a function of AI system autonomy, user reliance, and task complexity.
definition: The Monetary Authority of Singapore (MAS) is Singapore's central bank and integrated financial services regulator. Its November 2025 consultation paper on AI risk management proposes a calibration framework for human oversight: quantum of oversight = f(autonomy, reliance, complexity), where reliance is defined as the level of autonomy granted to the AI system, the degree of human involvement in the process it supports, and the availability of alternatives. The paper names the variables without resolving the function — calibration remains institution-by-institution, use-case-by-use-case. MAS is frequently cited alongside OSFI E-23 and PRA SS1/23 as a leading international reference for AI governance in banking.

short: The potential scope of harm from a security finding — financial exposure, data affected, downstream systems reachable, and regulatory consequence.
definition: In adversarial AI testing, blast radius is used to score and prioritize findings. A finding whose blast radius is limited to an internal audit log is a different severity class than one that can dispatch a wire transfer or exfiltrate OAuth-scoped data. The concept is borrowed from cloud security, where it describes the impact of a compromised credential or misconfigured service.

short: A structured process for enumerating what an adversary could reach in a system, through which mechanisms, and at which trust boundaries.
definition: In LLM and agentic AI deployments, threat modeling identifies each trust boundary — user input, retrieved content, tool outputs, memory, sub-agents, MCP servers — names the controlling party for each, and estimates the blast radius if that boundary is compromised. The recommended framework for agentic systems is STRIDE augmented with the OWASP Agentic Top 10 attack taxonomy. The output is a Trust Boundary Register that belongs in the Model Risk Committee documentation package.

short: A public catalog of known cybersecurity vulnerabilities maintained by MITRE, searchable at cve.org.
definition: CVE identifiers (e.g., CVE-2025-32711) provide a standardized reference for tracking and discussing publicly disclosed security vulnerabilities. In the LLM context, the 2024–2025 CVE record documents prompt injection attacks escalating to SQL injection, OS command execution, and OAuth-scoped data exfiltration — providing an empirical baseline for adversarial testing scope.

short: OWASP's 2026 taxonomy of the ten highest-risk attack classes for agentic AI systems (ASI01–ASI10): Agent Goal Hijack, Tool Misuse, Identity & Privilege Abuse, Supply Chain, Code Execution, Memory Poisoning, Inter-Agent Communication, Cascading Failures, Human-Agent Trust Exploitation, and Rogue Agents.
definition: Distinct from the OWASP LLM Top 10 (which covers language-model-specific risks), the Agentic Top 10 addresses risks that emerge when LLMs act as orchestrators or sub-agents with tool access. The ten categories: ASI01 Agent Goal Hijack (the primary vehicle for indirect prompt injection), ASI02 Tool Misuse & Exploitation, ASI03 Identity & Privilege Abuse, ASI04 Agentic Supply Chain Vulnerabilities, ASI05 Unexpected Code Execution, ASI06 Memory & Context Poisoning, ASI07 Insecure Inter-Agent Communication, ASI08 Cascading Failures, ASI09 Human-Agent Trust Exploitation, ASI10 Rogue Agents. The taxonomy is designed to cross-reference with MITRE ATLAS technique IDs for examiner-facing documentation.

short: Contamination of training data or retrieval corpora to steer model behavior toward attacker-controlled outputs.
definition: Data poisoning attacks target the inputs used to train or ground a model. PoisonedRAG is the retrieval-augmented variant: malicious documents inserted into a knowledge base (e.g., Confluence, SharePoint) cause the model to retrieve and act on hostile content. Training-time poisoning targets fine-tuning datasets. Both require different defenses and produce different risk profiles in a model risk framework.

short: Injection of persistent hostile directives into an agent's long-term memory store, causing the agent to behave maliciously across future sessions.
definition: Unlike prompt injection, which affects a single inference call, memory poisoning persists across sessions. Demonstrated attacks (e.g., against Gemini's memory layer) show that a single injected directive can alter an agent's behavior in all subsequent conversations with a user. This failure mode has no analogue in static models and is one of three attack classes (alongside indirect prompt injection and tool-call hijacking) that break traditional model-risk assumptions.

short: Query-based inference attacks that reconstruct a model's decision boundary by systematically probing its outputs.
definition: Model extraction allows an attacker to clone a proprietary model's behavior without access to its weights, by issuing many queries and training a surrogate on the responses. In banking, the concern is extraction of credit-scoring logic via adverse-action APIs — potentially enabling targeted applicants to game the model or enabling competitors to replicate proprietary underwriting.

full: Spoofing, Tampering, Repudiation, Information Disclosure, Denial of Service, Elevation of Privilege
short: A threat modeling framework covering six attack categories: Spoofing, Tampering, Repudiation, Information disclosure, Denial of service, and Elevation of privilege.
definition: Originally developed at Microsoft, STRIDE is a structured framework for enumerating threats during system design. For agentic AI systems, STRIDE is typically augmented with the OWASP Agentic Top 10 taxonomy to cover attack classes (indirect prompt injection, tool-call hijacking, memory poisoning) that don't map cleanly to the original six categories. The output of a STRIDE analysis is a threat register that feeds into a Trust Boundary Register for MRM documentation.

full: Generative AI Red-teaming and Assessment Kit
short: NVIDIA's open-source LLM red-teaming tool (Apache-2.0) that runs structured probe families against model deployments and produces JSONL regression logs.
definition: Garak executes probe families (including prompt injection, DAN jailbreaks, encoding-based attacks, and data leakage) against an LLM endpoint and scores each with detector classifiers. Probe scores are not normalized and not comparable across probe families — Garak's value is regression tracking within a single deployment over time, not cross-system benchmarking. It is the most commonly used open-source baseline tool for MRM adversarial testing programs.

full: Python Risk Identification Toolkit for Generative AI
short: Microsoft's open-source red-teaming framework providing orchestrators, scorers, and built-in attacks (Crescendo, TAP, PAIR, Skeleton Key, Many-Shot) for automated adversarial testing.
definition: PyRIT (arXiv:2410.02828) provides a modular architecture: orchestrators manage multi-turn attack strategies, converters transform prompts (encoding, translation, paraphrasing), targets wrap LLM endpoints, scorers evaluate whether attacks succeeded, and memory stores (SQLite or Azure SQL) persist results. Built-in attack strategies include Crescendo (multi-turn benign-to-harmful priming), TAP (Tree of Attacks with Pruning), PAIR, Skeleton Key, and Many-Shot Jailbreaking. The Scenarios CLI (`pyrit_scan`) supports 25+ strategies across easy/moderate/difficult tiers.

short: Bainbridge's 1983 observation that automating the routine parts of a task degrades human manual skill and vigilance precisely at the moments when intervention is most needed.
definition: Lisanne Bainbridge's foundational paper ('Ironies of Automation,' Automatica, 1983) identified a structural paradox: the more reliable an automated system, the less practice operators get with manual control, leaving them least capable of intervening at the rare moments when the automation fails. The paper is directly applicable to agentic AI oversight: model validation teams that never practice overriding well-performing models accumulate the same deficit. The 'ironies' are architectural — automation reliably creates the conditions for its own oversight failure.

short: The time period during which an action taken by an AI agent can still be undone or corrected — a key factor in determining where a runtime human-oversight checkpoint must sit.
definition: The reversibility window is the design variable that determines the urgency of runtime HITL checkpoints. For ACH payments (1–2 day settlement), the window is wide enough to allow post-execution review. For RTP/FedNow instant payments, the window is effectively zero — funds settle in seconds with no chargeback mechanism. Wire transfers and stablecoin transactions are similarly irrevocable. Knowing when an action remains reversible determines where the interrupt gate must sit; existence of a gate is a separate question from whether the gate is positioned before the irreversibility threshold.

short: A runtime intervention mechanism that intercepts, evaluates, and passes or blocks model behavior before it reaches a user or downstream system — distinct from tests and monitoring metrics.
definition: A guardrail operates on live model behavior, not historical aggregates. Input guardrails screen prompts for integrity (jailbreak attempts, social engineering); output guardrails filter responses for safety and reliability (PII redaction, hallucination checks); tool guardrails enforce execution boundaries (allowlists, RBAC); structural guardrails validate data schema for downstream systems. Behavioral guardrails — screening for prompt injection, jailbreaking, and goal hijacking — address model 'intent' and lack established validation frameworks under SR 11-7. In SR 11-7 terms, guardrail KPIs belong in Ongoing Monitoring; the guardrail mechanism itself is subject to Conceptual Soundness validation.

short: The practice of designing and maintaining persistent, version-controlled files (CLAUDE.md, AGENTS.md) that inject project knowledge into every agent session, replacing repetitive prompting.
definition: Context engineering solves the cold-start problem — where new agent sessions begin with no knowledge of the project, conventions, or prior decisions — by encoding that knowledge in version-controlled Markdown files. Key failure modes: staleness (projects change faster than context files), context window bloat (lost-in-the-middle effects from oversized files), and conflicting instructions across multi-tool setups. ETH Zurich research found LLM-generated context files can reduce task success rates by ~2% while increasing costs 20–23%, because agents follow bad instructions as faithfully as good ones.

short: A context window management strategy that summarizes earlier conversation turns in place to free token budget while preserving session continuity.
definition: Compaction is a harness-level technique for managing long agent sessions: earlier turns are summarized in place rather than truncated or reset. It preserves continuity but can introduce 'context anxiety' — the agent's tendency to rush or cut corners as it sees the window filling.
