The alternative is a full context reset paired with a rich handoff artifact. Which approach works better has evolved with model capability: Sonnet 4.5 required explicit resets; Opus 4.6 largely handles long sessions with automatic compaction.

short: A large-scale AI model trained on broad data and accessed via API — posing unique MRM challenges because banks cannot inspect internals, control versioning, or replicate outputs.
definition: Foundation models (e.g., Claude, GPT-4, Gemini) are pre-trained on massive datasets and served through APIs. Unlike in-house models, banks using foundation models cannot inspect underlying parameters, cannot control when the vendor updates the model, and cannot perform traditional replication-based validation. SR 11-7's vendor model provisions still apply — banks must take compensating steps when internals are inaccessible — but the compromise becomes far more consequential when the vendor is a frontier AI lab with unannounced update cycles.

short: The practice of designing, versioning, and managing system prompts and few-shot examples that shape an LLM's behavior — increasingly treated as model configuration requiring change management.
definition: Prompt engineering encompasses system prompt design, instruction framing, few-shot example selection, and temperature configuration. In an MRM context, these are model configuration choices analogous to hyperparameter settings and should be subject to version control, documentation, and change management. Researchers at Wells Fargo (arXiv 2503.15668) have proposed treating prompt engineering as a distinct documentation category under SR 11-7, separate from model architecture and training data documentation.

short: An attack or failure mode where an agent's objective is corrupted — through adversarial inputs, memory poisoning, or emergent drift — causing it to pursue unintended goals.
definition: Goal hijacking is distinct from prompt injection (redirects a single response) and jailbreaking (bypasses safety constraints for that session). In goal hijacking, the agent's underlying objective is durably corrupted — through adversarial content embedded in retrieved documents, through memory poisoning across sessions, or through emergent optimization toward a proxy goal. In financial services, the canonical example is an investment agent that gradually shifts toward higher-risk strategies not authorized by the user.

short: The orchestration layer that transforms a stateless LLM call into a persistent, tool-using agent — managing the execution loop, context window, error recovery, and inter-session state.
definition: An agent harness is the engineering infrastructure that gives an LLM persistence across turns, sessions, and tool calls. It is distinct from context files (which encode knowledge) and model capabilities (which determine reasoning quality). The harness enforces what the agent cannot do reliably as a model instruction alone: absolute tool prohibitions, context window monitoring, retry logic, human approval gates, and state persistence via structured files. Harness design is a primary determinant of agent reliability in production, independent of model capability — a well-designed harness running a smaller model can outperform a naive setup with a larger one.

short: A psychometric framework that models observed scores as true latent score plus random error — giving LLM evaluators a principled way to separate signal from noise across stochastic outputs.
definition: Classical Test Theory (CTT) models any observed measurement as the sum of a true score (what the test would measure across infinite replications) and random error. Applied to LLM evaluation, CTT provides a framework for handling non-deterministic outputs: repeated sampling of a model on the same prompt estimates the 'true score' for that prompt, and variance across samples characterizes measurement noise. CTT complements Item Response Theory (IRT) — IRT models item difficulty and discrimination; CTT models reliability across replications.

short: The challenge of tracing which agent in a multi-agent chain was causally responsible for a harmful outcome — with direct regulatory implications for explainability requirements under ECOA and GDPR.
definition: In multi-agent AI systems, the credit assignment problem refers to the difficulty of attributing a final outcome — helpful or harmful — to the specific actions of individual agents in a delegation chain. When a pipeline of agents collectively produces a decision, no established method exists for determining which agent's contribution was decisive. This has direct regulatory implications: ECOA requires specific denial reasons, GDPR requires explainability, and fair lending frameworks require traceability. The problem compounds when agents themselves use other agents as tools.

short: Running an alternative model in parallel with a production model to continuously compare performance and support evidence-based deployment decisions.
definition: A model governance practice where a current production model (the champion) is run alongside one or more candidate alternatives (challengers) on live or representative data. Performance differences inform promotion or retirement decisions and provide an ongoing evidence record for model risk management. Under SR 11-7 and its successors, champion-challenger testing is considered a best practice for high-criticality models. As a continuous rather than periodic mechanism, it addresses non-determinism and drift in ways that point-in-time validation cannot.

short: A structured adversarial exercise in which a tester attempts to exploit vulnerabilities in a target system to assess its real-world security posture.
definition: Unlike automated vulnerability scanning, penetration testing involves human-driven creative attack chains — chaining together multiple weaknesses to achieve an objective. In the LLM context, a penetration test of a deployed model or agent system differs from safety benchmarking: it probes exploitability against a specific deployment environment, not capability regression in the abstract. For MRM purposes, an adversarial red-team engagement is conceptually equivalent to a penetration test applied to the model's trust boundaries, tool interfaces, and prompt surfaces.

short: A rubric-based LLM safety benchmark (NeurIPS 2024) designed to correct for overcounting in earlier jailbreak benchmarks by scoring refusal quality, not just binary harm presence.
definition: Introduced by Souly et al. (NeurIPS 2024) in response to documented failures of binary jailbreak classifiers — particularly the Scots Gaelic / low-resource jailbreak case where incoherent output was counted as successful harm. StrongREJECT evaluates three dimensions: refusal (binary), convincingness (1–5), and specificity (1–5). It achieves the highest human-agreement scores of published safety judges. In MRM deployment gates, it functions as a safety non-regression benchmark for comparing a candidate model against the champion on the same rubric across releases.

short: A large-scale adversarial safety benchmark (ICML 2024) with 510 curated behaviors, comparing 18 attack methods against 33 LLMs to track refusal regression across model versions.
definition: Developed by the Center for AI Safety and published at ICML 2024. HarmBench provides 510 curated harmful behaviors across standard, copyright, contextual, and multimodal categories. Its key finding for deployment gate design is that Attack Success Rate (ASR) is stable within model families but variable across them — meaning HarmBench gates must be parameterized per model family rather than as a global threshold. Primarily useful as a regression-tracking workhorse: detecting whether a new model version has degraded on harm categories the prior version handled.

short: The EU's comprehensive regulatory framework for crypto-assets, covering stablecoins, crypto-asset service providers, and market integrity; stablecoin rules applicable June 2024, full CASP rules December 2024.
definition: Regulation EU 2023/1114, adopted by Parliament on April 20, 2023, signed May 31, 2023, and published in the Official Journal of the EU in June 2023 (when it entered into force). MiCA is the first comprehensive EU framework for crypto-assets. Stablecoin rules (asset-referenced tokens and e-money tokens) became applicable June 30, 2024; rules for crypto-asset service providers (CASPs) became fully applicable December 30, 2024. Key provisions for significant non-EUR stablecoins include hard transaction caps (a monetary sovereignty measure), reserve requirements, and authorization requirements for issuers and service providers. Contrasted with the U.S. GENIUS Act, which passed in July 2025 and focuses on reserve and chartering requirements without equivalent consumer protection mandates for fraud recovery.

short: An AI agent that interacts with software applications through their visual interface — clicking buttons, filling forms, reading screenshots — without requiring API or programmatic access.
definition: A class of AI agent that operates a computer or browser the way a human would: by perceiving screenshots, identifying interactive elements, and emitting click, type, and scroll actions. Unlike API-based agents that call structured endpoints, GUI agents can operate any software with a visual interface — making them potentially more general but also more dependent on visual grounding accuracy. In production contexts, GUI agents face specific challenges: OCR failures on small text, stale DOM state, authentication flows outside training distributions, and the pixel-to-semantics interpretation gap that synthetic AxTree-based training teachers don't have. Relevant to banking deployments for internal portal automation and customer-facing workflow assistance.

short: A measure of a bond or loan's sensitivity to interest rate changes, expressed in years; longer-duration assets carry greater interest rate risk because their cash flows are locked in further into the future.
definition: Duration quantifies how much the price of a fixed-income instrument changes for a given change in interest rates. A 10-year mortgage has far higher duration than a 30-day repo — meaning the bank holding it is exposed to much larger mark-to-market losses if rates rise. In Funds Transfer Pricing, duration is the key dimension used to match each loan or deposit to the correct point on the internal funding curve, ensuring the business unit pays — or receives credit for — the full term premium embedded in its product.

short: The additional return or internal rate charge required to compensate for holding or funding assets that cannot easily be converted to cash; in FTP, expressed as a surcharge on products that demand immediate, on-demand access to funds.
definition: A liquidity premium reflects the cost of committing funds to uses that cannot be quickly unwound. In bank FTP frameworks, products that draw on demand (like demand deposits funding mortgages) carry a liquidity surcharge because they expose the treasury to unexpected outflow risk. In the token pricing analogy, synchronous real-time inference imposes a liquidity premium over scheduled batch inference — the platform must hold excess capacity available on demand rather than scheduling it predictably.

short: Internal bank pricing mechanism that charges business units for the funds they consume based on duration, liquidity, and optionality — making cross-subsidies between desks visible and correctable through price signals rather than mandates.
definition: FTP assigns each loan or deposit an internal cost or credit based on the true cost of funding it, adjusted for duration, liquidity risk, and embedded optionality. It prevents business units from exploiting gaps in the bank's internal funding curve — for example, booking long-duration loans while pocketing the term premium without paying the corresponding duration risk. The mechanism transforms treasury policy into a price signal that automatically shapes business unit behavior without requiring explicit mandates.

short: The gold-standard FTP variant where each product is priced against the specific point on the internal funding curve that matches its duration, eliminating cross-subsidization between short- and long-term products.
definition: In matched maturity FTP, every loan or deposit is assigned an internal transfer rate corresponding to a point on the bank's funding curve with the same tenor or duration. A 30-year mortgage is priced against the 30-year internal curve, not a blended average rate — which means the mortgage desk cannot benefit from a steep yield curve without paying the term premium in its funding charge. This is considered the gold standard because it eliminates duration mismatches at the product level.

short: The risk that two correlated financial instruments move at different rates, leaving a residual exposure even after an apparently hedged position. In FTP, the treasury absorbs basis risk so business units can plan against stable internal rates.
definition: Basis risk arises when a hedging instrument does not move in perfect lockstep with the underlying exposure — for example, when the rate used to fund a loan drifts relative to the rate earned on that loan. In the context of bank treasury operations and FTP, the treasury function absorbs basis risk from external market volatility, offering business units stable internal transfer prices. In the token context, the central AI platform absorbs basis risk from external API price changes so that internal departments face predictable internal charges.

short: Short-term interest rates set by repurchase agreements, serving as a critical benchmark for overnight and short-term liquidity in bank funding markets.
definition: Repo rates are the interest rates implied by repurchase agreements, in which a borrower sells securities and agrees to repurchase them at a higher price on a specified date. They function as a key short-term funding benchmark: when wholesale funding tightens, repo rates spike, immediately raising the cost of short-duration borrowing for banks. In the FTP context, a repo rate spike triggers the treasury to reprice internal transfer rates — transmitting wholesale market stress directly to business desk economics without requiring an explicit mandate. The mortgage desk's spread compresses automatically because the internal funding rate moved.

short: A cloud financial management discipline that allocates and optimizes infrastructure spend at the team and feature level, rather than treating it as undifferentiated IT overhead.
definition: FinOps emerged as a practice when cloud spend grew large enough that pooled IT overhead accounting became inadequate. It applies unit economics thinking to cloud infrastructure: which team consumed which resources, which features drove cost, and whether the spend was justified by the value produced. The FinOps Foundation formalized the practice, and it is now a standard function at organizations with significant cloud workloads. Token economics for LLMs is following a similar trajectory, with the key difference that the cost driver is behavioral (prompt design, model selection, caching choices) rather than provisioned resource allocation.

short: Behavioral gaming of AI usage metrics — redundant prompting, task inflation, delegation theater — that emerges when token consumption becomes a KPI rather than a cost tied to outcomes. Goodhart's Law at the compute layer.
definition: Token Maxing describes the pattern in which teams or individuals optimize AI usage metrics — prompts per day, tokens consumed, AI interactions logged — rather than the outcomes those metrics were intended to proxy. When AI-integration scores become performance targets, the rational response is to maximize the metric through redundant prompting, inflated task delegation, and other forms of usage theater. The fix is the same as in banking's FTP parallel: replace activity metrics with cost-per-outcome metrics, so that the denominator is outcome value, not consumption volume.

short: A structured browser representation of a page's elements — their labels, roles, and relationships — exposed by the browser for assistive technologies and, in agent contexts, readable by text-only agents as an alternative to pixel-level screenshot inference.
definition: The accessibility tree (often abbreviated AxTree) is a hierarchical data structure the browser maintains alongside the visual DOM. It maps each page element to a semantic label, role (button, link, input), and state (checked, disabled, focused). Screen readers use it; so do text-based web agents. In the MolmoWeb training pipeline, Gemini agents read the AxTree rather than screenshots, giving them direct access to element semantics that the trained visual model won't have at inference time — the source of the information asymmetry at the heart of the synthetic-vs-human ablation.

short: The browser's in-memory tree representation of an HTML document — the structured source from which both the visual render and the accessibility tree are derived.
definition: The Document Object Model (DOM) is the browser's programmatic representation of a web page as a tree of nodes (elements, attributes, text). JavaScript reads and mutates the DOM; CSS styles it; the browser renders it visually. The accessibility tree is derived from the DOM but is not identical to it — not every DOM node has an accessible role, and the AxTree reflects semantic intent rather than raw markup structure. Web agents that read the DOM directly are brittle to sites that generate markup dynamically or use non-semantic elements; agents that read the accessibility tree are more robust but still miss canvas-rendered or PDF-based content.

short: A prompting framework where an agent alternates Reasoning steps and Action calls in a structured loop, enabling step-by-step tool use with explicit thought traces before each action.
definition: A prompting framework where an agent alternates Reasoning steps and Action calls in a structured loop, enabling step-by-step tool use with explicit thought traces before each action.

short: An evaluation metric measuring the probability that at least one of k model attempts at a task succeeds; often reported as pass@1 (single attempt) or pass@4 (best of four parallel rollouts).
definition: An evaluation metric measuring the probability that at least one of k model attempts at a task succeeds; often reported as pass@1 (single attempt) or pass@4 (best of four parallel rollouts).

short: Vision-Language Model: a foundation model capable of processing both images and text as input, enabling visual reasoning, screenshot understanding, and GUI agent behavior.
definition: Vision-Language Model: a foundation model capable of processing both images and text as input, enabling visual reasoning, screenshot understanding, and GUI agent behavior.

short: An open-source browser automation library by Microsoft that controls Chromium, Firefox, and WebKit programmatically; used by GUI agents to execute web actions (click, type, navigate) and capture screenshots.
definition: An open-source browser automation library by Microsoft that controls Chromium, Firefox, and WebKit programmatically; used by GUI agents to execute web actions (click, type, navigate) and capture screenshots.

short: A permissive open-source software license that allows use, modification, and distribution with minimal restrictions, including in commercial products, provided attribution is given.
definition: A permissive open-source software license that allows use, modification, and distribution with minimal restrictions, including in commercial products, provided attribution is given.

short: Open Data Commons Attribution License: a permissive open data license requiring attribution, commonly used for releasing machine learning datasets alongside Apache-licensed code.
definition: Open Data Commons Attribution License: a permissive open data license requiring attribution, commonly used for releasing machine learning datasets alongside Apache-licensed code.

short: Key-value cache: an inference optimization that stores intermediate attention computations across tokens so they need not be recomputed on each new generation step; absence of KV-cache reuse across agent steps means full re-encoding every step.
definition: Key-value cache: an inference optimization that stores intermediate attention computations across tokens so they need not be recomputed on each new generation step; absence of KV-cache reuse across agent steps means full re-encoding every step.

short: An open-ended web agent benchmark of 643 real-world tasks across 15 websites, evaluated by GPT-4V as judge; a primary benchmark for GUI agent capability with pass@1 and pass@4 metrics.
definition: An open-ended web agent benchmark of 643 real-world tasks across 15 websites, evaluated by GPT-4V as judge; a primary benchmark for GUI agent capability with pass@1 and pass@4 metrics.

short: A live web agent benchmark derived from Mind2Web, requiring agents to complete tasks on real websites rather than static snapshots; tests generalization to dynamic, unanticipated web states.
definition: A live web agent benchmark derived from Mind2Web, requiring agents to complete tasks on real websites rather than static snapshots; tests generalization to dynamic, unanticipated web states.
